articles.topic: daily multivitamin biological aging epigenetic clocks
articles.headline: Your Daily Multivitamin Might Be Doing More Than You Think
articles.body: A large randomized clinical trial from Harvard and Mass General Brigham found that taking a daily multivitamin for two years slowed biological aging by roughly four months, as measured by DNA-based epigenetic clocks. The study analyzed nearly 1,000 adults from the COSMOS trial. Two of the five aging clocks showed statistically significant improvement in the multivitamin group versus placebo. People who were biologically older than their actual age at the start of the study benefited the most. Cocoa extract, tested alongside the multivitamin, showed no effect on any of the five clocks. Researchers say more work is needed to understand whether the slowdown translates to real-world health outcomes.
articles.takeaway: A basic daily multivitamin is a low-cost, safe addition worth considering, especially if your diet is inconsistent or you're over 50.

product.name: Thorne Vitamin D3/K2
product.blurb: Most adults are deficient. D3 with K2 is the combination that actually works.

articles.topic: daily steps weight regain prevention walking
articles.headline: The Step Count That Keeps the Weight Off After You Lose It
articles.body: Losing weight is hard. Keeping it off is harder. New research presented at the European Congress on Obesity found that walking around 8,500 steps per day may be the key to stopping weight from creeping back after dieting. The study, a systematic review and meta-analysis, found that people who built up their daily steps during a weight loss program and kept that activity level going afterward were significantly better at maintaining their results. Interestingly, walking more did not seem to drive more weight loss during the dieting phase itself. Researchers think that step count becomes a stronger tool during the maintenance phase.
articles.takeaway: If you've lost weight and want to keep it off, aim for around 8,500 steps a day as your maintenance target.

articles.topic: positive psychology mindfulness cardiovascular risk factors
articles.headline: Gratitude Journals and Mindfulness Can Actually Move the Needle on Heart Health
articles.body: It sounds soft, but the data is getting harder to ignore. A new review published in Cardiology Clinics analyzed 18 randomized controlled trials and found that positive psychology interventions like mindfulness, gratitude journaling, and optimism training can improve blood pressure, lower inflammation markers, and reduce other cardiovascular risk factors within weeks. The catch: the benefits faded when programs stopped. Programs with more frequent contact with participants produced the most consistent results. The strongest improvements came from an eight-week program that combined weekly sessions with daily micro-tasks.
articles.takeaway: Short, consistent mindfulness or gratitude practices are not just mood boosters. They may meaningfully support your heart health over time.

articles.topic: optimism heart disease cardiovascular risk outlook
articles.headline: Thinking Positively About the Future Is Actually Good for Your Heart
articles.body: A growing body of research now links optimism directly to better cardiovascular outcomes. Studies show that more optimistic people tend to have healthier hearts, both because of biological pathways and because they are more likely to exercise, eat well, and stick to health behaviors. A major meta-analysis of 229,000 people found that optimistic individuals had a 35 percent lower risk of experiencing a cardiovascular event compared to their more pessimistic peers. The Washington Post this week rounded up the current science on the topic, noting that researchers believe optimism is a modifiable trait, not a fixed personality feature.
articles.takeaway: Working on your outlook is not just mental health self-care. It may be one of the most underrated things you can do for your heart.

quote.text: The body achieves what the mind believes.
quote.author: Napoleon Hill
quote.context: American author and pioneer of the modern self-help movement, best known for Think and Grow Rich.

workout.title: Lunch Break Ladder: 1-2-3 Minute Interval Pyramid
workout.tip: Keep your easy jog recovery truly easy. If you can not hold a short conversation during recovery, you are going too hard on the work intervals.
workout.warmup: Start with 5 minutes of easy walking or a very light jog. Then do 30 seconds of high knees and 30 seconds of butt kicks to wake up your legs.

exercises.name: Easy jog
exercises.notes: Conversational pace. This is your base effort before intervals begin.

exercises.name: 1-minute hard effort
exercises.notes: Run at a pace that feels comfortably hard, like a 7 out of 10. Recover with a slow jog or walk in between.

exercises.name: 2-minute hard effort
exercises.notes: Same effort level as the 1-minute block. Focus on keeping your form tall and relaxed as fatigue sets in.

exercises.name: 3-minute hard effort
exercises.notes: This is the peak of the pyramid. Dig in and hold your pace steady throughout the full 3 minutes.

workout.cooldown: Finish with 5 minutes of easy walking. Do a standing quad stretch and a gentle calf stretch on each leg to close out.

product.name: HOKA Clifton 9
product.blurb: Max cushion for easy days and long runs. Keeps your legs fresh for hard sessions.

meal.title: Crispy Tofu and Edamame Rice Bowl with Sesame Ginger Dressing
ingredients: 1 cup cooked brown rice or microwaveable rice pouch
2 tbsp low-sodium soy sauce or tamari
1 tsp fresh grated ginger (or 1/2 tsp ground ginger)
1 tsp olive oil or avocado oil for cooking
meal.instructions: 1. Pat tofu dry with paper towels and cube into bite-sized pieces. Heat oil in a non-stick skillet over medium-high heat and cook tofu for 3-4 minutes per side until golden and crispy. Season lightly with soy sauce in the last minute. 2. While tofu cooks, whisk together sesame oil, rice vinegar, remaining soy sauce, ginger, and honey to make the dressing. 3. Build the bowl: scoop rice into a bowl, top with edamame, cabbage, carrot, and crispy tofu. 4. Drizzle dressing over the top, then finish with sesame seeds and green onion.
meal.tip: Press your tofu for at least 10 minutes beforehand using a heavy pan or paper towels. Drier tofu crisps up much better and holds its shape in the pan.

product.name: Greater Goods Digital Food Scale
product.blurb: Measure your food for two weeks and you will understand your body better than any diet book.
